_full_text:
Summary of Gender-Based Analysis (GBA) Plus assessment
For project: Automated decision-making on applications for Temporary
Resident Visas and Work Permits made under the Canada-Ukraine
Authorization for Emergency Travel
Launch date: September 26, 2022
The purpose of this Summary for public disclosure is to provide the findings of the GBA
Plus assessment for public consumption as related to automation solutions, and to
explain how these findings have informed the proposed options and approach.
Project Overview
The Canada-Ukraine Authorization for Emergency Travel (CUAET) was introduced in March
2022 as a temporary measure to support Ukrainians fleeing the war. To manage the high
volume of applications for Temporary Resident Visas (TRVs) and Work Permits (WPs),
Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada (IRCC) implemented automation within the
Global Case Management System (GCMS). This change enabled the use of existing
automation functionalities to finalize approvals on applications under the CUAET measure.
Automation was used to process simple applications to finalization, allowing officers to
focus on more value-added work requiring human intervention. The GBA Plus analysis was
conducted in September 2022 to examine the impacts of this automation on applicants’
identity criteria and to identify and mitigate any negative impacts. The CUAET program
concluded in 2023, with final client travel permitted until March 2024.
Objective and Scope of GBA Plus Analysis
The purpose of this GBA Plus report was to describe the potential impacts of using
automation in GCMS for processing applications under the CUAET measure. The objective
was to identify any potential concerns on equity and propose mitigation measures. The
analysis focused on the impacts based on identity factors caused by automation in GCMS. It
did not assess impacts from manual processing or regular officer decision-making, although
manual processing statistics were used as a baseline for monitoring equity-related impacts.
The assessment examined client outcomes based on identity criteria such as gender, age,
and nationality. As of September 2022, over 560,000 clients had submitted applications
under the program, with a high approval rate. The analysis was limited to the automation of
TRVs and WPs, which used fully electronic applications.
1
Methodology
The GBA Plus used data from the Global Case Management System to examine the impacts
of automation on applicants under the CUAET measure. The analysis focused on identity
criteria such as gender, age, and nationality. Disaggregated data was used to assess refusal
rates across these groups, including comparisons before and after the implementation of
automation. Tables summarizing refusal rates by gender and age were developed and used
as baselines for monitoring.
This approach was selected to identify any differential outcomes that could be linked to the
automation rules. While gender was not used in decision-making, it was monitored to assess
potential impacts. Age and nationality were used as criteria in business rules for assessment
to ensure program eligibility and the safety of minors, in alignment with the Hague
Convention and biometrics exemptions.
Applications that did not meet automation rules dropped out for manual review, ensuring
that complex or exceptional cases received officer attention. This methodology allowed IRCC
to assess whether automation introduced any unintended bias or barriers for equity-seeking
groups.
Limitations
There were a few limitations identified in this GBA Plus. Some identity groups, such as
clients identifying as a gender other than male or female, had small sample sizes, which
limited the ability to draw statistically significant conclusions. For example, while refusal
rates appeared higher for applicants who did not identify as male or female, this was due to
the low total number of applications received.
Language barriers were also identified as a potential limitation. As English and French are
not commonly spoken in Ukraine, some applicants may have faced challenges
understanding and completing the online application forms. While this could have led to
errors and increased the likelihood of applications dropping out of automation, the system
was designed to refer such cases for officer review, ensuring fairness. This process mirrored
manual review procedures and helped mitigate the impact of language-related issues.
Finally, family composition was initially a factor in automation rules, with family group
applications dropping out for manual review to allow for simultaneous decision-making.
While this rule was later removed to improve processing efficiency, IRCC monitored for any
divergence in processing times between family members to ensure that no unintended
impacts occurred.
2
Summary of Results
As of the conclusion of the CUAET program, a total of 817,564 applications had been
received from 568,109 clients, including 147,734 family groups. Of these, 113,383
applications (TRVs and WPs combined) were processed through automation.
The GBA Plus analysis found no evidence of disproportionate impacts on equity-seeking
groups resulting from the use of automation. Refusal rates across identity groups remained
low overall, and the use of automation did not appear to introduce systemic bias.
Refusal rates by age group were all below 0.5%, with the 61+ age group showing slightly
lower rates than the 0–15 and 16–60 groups. This was considered expected for this
demographic and did not indicate any concerning trend.
In terms of nationality, approximately 4.5% of CUAET applicants were not Ukrainian
nationals. This group had a significantly higher refusal rate than Ukrainian nationals, which
was anticipated given the program’s eligibility criteria. The automation system was designed
to refer non-Ukrainian applicants for manual review to confirm eligibility, helping to ensure
fairness in decision-making.
Gender-based analysis showed that refusal rates for both male and female applicants
remained low, under 1.5%. However, refusal rates for male applicants were slightly higher
than for female applicants, particularly in the 16–60 age group. Applicants identifying as a
gender other than male or female had a significantly higher refusal rate, though this was
attributed to the small sample size, which limited the ability to draw meaningful conclusions.
While a comparison of pre- and post-automation outcomes was not conducted for work
permit applications, TRV refusal rates for both women and men appeared to decrease
following the implementation of automation. However, the difference in refusal rates
between genders remained.
Initially, a business rule required applications submitted as part of a family group to drop out
of automation so that all members could be processed together. This rule was later removed
to improve processing efficiency. This change was not expected to impact the decision
outcomes themselves, and it was considered unlikely that minor children would receive
approvals before their parents. To ensure no unintended impacts occurred, monitoring was
put in place to observe any divergence in processing times between family members.
Language barriers were acknowledged as a potential challenge for some applicants, but the
automation system was designed to refer such cases for officer review, mitigating the risk of
negative outcomes.
Overall, the results of the GBA Plus assessment supported the conclusion that the
automation functioned as intended and did not negatively affect client outcomes based on
identity factors.
3
Ongoing Monitoring & Next Steps
As of 2025, all planned quality assurance and monitoring activities related to the automation
of CUAET applications have been completed. These activities were conducted following the
launch of automated approvals in 2022 and continued until the conclusion of the CUAET
program in 2023, with final client travel permitted until March 2024.
The monitoring included targeted quality assurance exercises to assess whether automation
was functioning as intended and to identify any unintended impacts on clients based on
identity factors. These exercises also incorporated intersectional analysis, where feasible, to
ensure that no equity-seeking groups were disproportionately affected.
4
